frameworks.name: prefrontal-amygdala top-down modulation
frameworks.one_line: ventromedial prefrontal cortex regulates amygdala via inhibitory functional connectivity

frameworks.name: resting-state functional connectivity
frameworks.one_line: intrinsic fMRI coupling indexes emotion-regulation circuitry without task

frameworks.name: sleep deprivation and emotional functioning
frameworks.one_line: prior work links sleep loss to reduced emotional intelligence and disturbance

core_question: Do typical (non-deprivation) variations in self-reported sleep duration relate to prefrontal-amygdala connectivity and emotional functioning?
key_assumptions: self-reported prior-night sleep approximates relevant sleep variation
negative vmPFC-amygdala connectivity reflects top-down emotion regulation
EQ-i, MSCEIT, and PAI tap distinct facets of emotional and psychological functioning
manipulations: individual differences in self-reported prior-night sleep duration
trait emotional intelligence (EQ-i) vs ability EI (MSCEIT) vs psychopathology (PAI)
key_findings: longer self-reported sleep associated with higher EQ-i scores across all scales
no association between sleep and ability emotional intelligence (MSCEIT)
longer sleep linked to lower symptom severity on half of PAI psychopathology scales
longer sleep linked to stronger negative right vmPFC-amygdala connectivity
interpretations: everyday sleep curtailment, not only deprivation, modulates emotion-regulation circuitry
stronger negative vmPFC-amygdala coupling indexes better emotional functioning
